title: Spider-Man: Brand New Day First Reactions — Critics Are Already Calling It Marvel's Best in Years
excerpt: Press screenings for Spider-Man: Brand New Day just wrapped — and the early reactions flooding social media are extraordinary. Critics are praising Tom Holland's career-best performance and calling this Marvel's most emotionally resonant film in years. One week to go.
author: StarScoop Daily Staff
imageAlt: Spider-Man: Brand New Day — Tom Holland returns as Peter Parker one week before release
metaDescription: Spider-Man: Brand New Day first reactions are in — critics call it Tom Holland's career-best and Marvel's most emotionally grounded film in years. In cinemas July 31, 2026.
content:
The Embargo Just Lifted — And the Internet Is Erupting

With exactly one week until Spider-Man: Brand New Day opens in cinemas on July 31, 2026, press screenings have wrapped and the social media embargo has lifted. The early reactions are flooding in — and they are overwhelming.

This is one of the most anticipated Marvel films in years, carrying the enormous emotional weight of No Way Home's gut-punch ending. The pressure on this film was immense. Based on what critics who attended the press screenings are saying, it appears Marvel has delivered something genuinely special.

What Critics Are Saying

The overwhelming early consensus is that Brand New Day is the best MCU film since Avengers: Endgame. Multiple critics are describing it as emotionally devastating, visually grounded, and a return to form for superhero cinema at a moment when audience fatigue had started to bite hard.

Words appearing repeatedly across the first reaction wave: "grounded", "human", "heartbreaking" — and notably, "brave". Several critics have pointed out that this film takes genuine risks with the character that most superhero movies simply wouldn't attempt. It's clear Marvel made a deliberate choice to strip things back after the multiverse chaos of the last few years.

One recurring theme in the early reactions is how Brand New Day handles the memory-loss premise from No Way Home. Rather than using it as a convenient plot reset, the film reportedly leans fully into the emotional devastation of that premise — and the result feels affecting rather than mechanical.

Tom Holland's Performance Has Critics Stunned

Perhaps the biggest talking point in the first reaction wave isn't the action sequences, the villain, or the post-credits scene — it's Tom Holland's acting. Critics who attended the press screenings are calling this a career-best performance, with several noting that he's finally been given material that asks him to do something more than charm an audience.

Holland, now 30, is playing a Peter Parker who has lost everything and is rebuilding from scratch. That's a very different emotional register from the bright, excitable teenager of the Homecoming era — and by every early account, he has risen to the challenge completely.

The scenes between Peter and a Zendaya-played MJ who has no memory of him are being specifically highlighted as some of the most emotionally complex sequences Marvel has ever produced. Zendaya's performance is drawing significant praise too — playing a character who has no memory of an important relationship turns out to be just as demanding as playing the character who remembers everything.

How Does It Compare to No Way Home?

Spider-Man: No Way Home is one of the most beloved superhero films ever made — a nostalgia-fuelled event movie that brought three generations of Spider-Men together for one enormous emotional payoff. Comparisons between Brand New Day and No Way Home are inevitable, and the early reactions address them directly.

Brand New Day is a very different film. More intimate, more focused, and deliberately stripped of the spectacle and nostalgia that made No Way Home what it was. That's not a flaw — it's a choice, and according to critics, it's the right one.

When the stakes are emotional rather than cosmic, the film apparently hits harder. Several critics have described Brand New Day as feeling more personal than any previous MCU Spider-Man entry. Peter Parker alone, anonymous, trying to figure out who he is without the people who defined him — that's a genuinely interesting story, and it sounds like Marvel told it well.

The Villain and the Post-Credits Scene

Most critics attending press screenings are respecting the spoiler embargo on the villain's identity — but what they are saying is that the antagonist works. The threat feels personal rather than world-ending, and the conflict connects directly to Peter's new anonymous life in a way that feels earned rather than convenient.

After years of universe-ending threats and multiverse chaos, a villain whose conflict with Spider-Man operates on a smaller, more human scale sounds like exactly what the franchise needed. On the post-credits scene — critics are confirming one exists and are saying almost nothing else. Which means it's significant.

Should You Trust the Hype?

Early social media reactions are notoriously unreliable. Studios curate their press screening audiences carefully, and the first wave often skews positive. Full reviews are embargoed until closer to release. That said, the specific things being praised for Brand New Day — Holland's performance, the emotional restraint, the grounded storytelling — aren't the kind of compliments that evaporate after a full critical rollout.

Spider-Man: Brand New Day is in cinemas from July 31, 2026. If you want to get ready, read our full breakdown of everything confirmed about Brand New Day before you buy your ticket — and check the latest from Hollywood for more coverage as release day approaches.
